content.problemStatement:
You are given two arrays AAA and BBB of size NNN. Given a function FFF such that. F(i,j)=Ai+BjF(i,j) = A_i  + B_jF(i,j)=Ai​+Bj​
Reorder the arrays AAA and BBB such that F(i,j)≥F(j,i)F(i,j) \geq F(j,i)F(i,j)≥F(j,i),  1≤i≤N,1≤j≤N,i<j1 \leq i \leq N, 1 \leq j \leq N, i \lt j1≤i≤N,1≤j≤N,i<j.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case will contain three lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers, A1,A2...ANA_1, A_2 . . . A_NA1​,A2​...AN​.
The third line of each test case contains NNN integers, B1,B2...BNB_1, B_2 . . . B_NB1​,B2​...BN​.


Output Format

In the first line of output, print the rearranged array AAA.
In the first line of output, print the rearranged array BBB.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with integer N, the size of the arrays.
- A line with N integers A1 … AN.
- A line with N integers B1 … BN.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output two lines:
- First line: the array A reordered so that the required condition holds (elements separated by a single space).
- Second line: the array B reordered correspondingly (elements separated by a single space).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Sorting A descending gives 8 5 3 1. Sorting B ascending gives 1 2 4 6. The condition A_i + B_j >= A_j + B_i holds for every i < j.
With a single element the condition is vacuously true; the arrays remain unchanged after sorting.
A sorted descending: 9 4 2. B sorted ascending: 3 6 8. All pairwise inequalities are satisfied.

solution.approach:
For any i < j the condition A_i + B_j >= A_j + B_i can be rewritten as (A_i - B_i) >= (A_j - B_j). Therefore the sequence of differences D_i = A_i - B_i must be non‑increasing. To achieve this we can freely permute the two arrays. The optimal way is to pair the largest values of A with the smallest values of B, the second largest A with the second smallest B, and so on. This is done by sorting A in descending order and B in ascending order. The resulting D_i are automatically sorted in non‑increasing order, satisfying the condition for every pair i < j.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N, the arrays A and B.
2. Sort A in descending order.
3. Sort B in ascending order.
4. Output the two sorted arrays.
The algorithm is greedy and provably correct because any other pairing would produce a smaller (or equal) difference for some earlier position, breaking the required ordering.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the two arrays.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().strip().split()))
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        A = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        B = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        A.sort(reverse=True)
        B.sort()
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, A)))
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, B)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
